title: Security Deposit Norms
text: In India, security deposits for residential rental agreements typically range from 2 to 3 months rent. Under the Model Tenancy Act 2021, the maximum permissible security deposit for residential premises is 2 months rent, and for non-residential premises it is 6 months advance. Demanding more than 3 months is above the standard norm. The landlord must return the full security deposit within 1 month of the tenant vacating, after making only documented deductions for damages beyond normal wear and tear. Failure to return within this period makes the landlord liable to pay interest.

title: Lock-in Period Norms
text: In India, the standard and most common lock-in period for residential rental agreements is 11 months. Agreements of 12 months or more require mandatory registration under the Registration Act 1908, involving stamp duty and registration fees. Lock-in periods exceeding 11 months are uncommon for residential rentals. A 24 or 36 month lock-in clause is significantly above the standard norm and places considerable financial burden on the tenant, who may face full remaining rent liability if they need to vacate early.

title: Notice Period Requirements
text: For residential rental agreements in India, the standard notice period for termination is typically 1 month (30 days). Under the Model Tenancy Act 2021, the required notice period for termination of a periodic tenancy is one rental period or one month, whichever is less. Notice periods exceeding 2 months are above the general standard. Any clause requiring a longer notice period from the tenant than from the landlord creates asymmetric obligation. Notice must be given in writing to be legally enforceable.

title: Rent Increase Clauses
text: A standard rent increase provision typically allows for a 5 to 10 percent annual increase. Under the Model Tenancy Act 2021, any increase in rent is permissible but must be as per the terms agreed in the tenancy agreement. Clauses allowing rent increases greater than 10 percent per year or more frequently than once per year are considered aggressive. Any rent increase must be communicated in writing with at least 3 months notice before the increase takes effect.

title: Landlord Right to Enter Premises
text: Under the Model Tenancy Act 2021, the landlord must give the tenant at least 24 hours written notice before entering the premises for inspection or repairs. Entry is permitted only at reasonable times, typically during daytime hours. Clauses granting the landlord unrestricted right of entry at any time without notice violate the tenant's right to peaceful enjoyment of the property and are considered overly invasive.

title: Subletting Rules
text: In India, a tenant generally cannot sublet the rented premises to another person without the explicit written consent of the landlord. Doing so without permission can be grounds for eviction. Under the Model Tenancy Act 2021, subletting without the landlord's written consent is a breach of the tenancy agreement.

title: Maintenance and Repair Responsibilities
text: Under the Model Tenancy Act 2021, the landlord is responsible for structural repairs, maintaining the property in habitable condition, ensuring water supply and sanitation, and repairing damage beyond the tenant's control. The tenant is responsible for minor day-to-day maintenance. Clauses that shift major structural repair responsibilities to the tenant are unusual and potentially unfair.

title: Eviction Grounds
text: Under the Model Tenancy Act 2021, a landlord can seek eviction only on specific legal grounds including: non-payment of rent for 2 or more months, subletting without permission, misusing the premises, causing damage to the property, the landlord requiring it for personal use, and the premises being unsafe. Eviction must be carried out through the Rent Authority or court. Self-help eviction, including cutting utilities or changing locks, is illegal.

title: Agreement Registration Requirements
text: In India, a rental agreement for a period of 12 months or more must be compulsorily registered under the Registration Act 1908. Agreements of less than 12 months are exempt. An unregistered agreement of 12 months or more is not admissible as evidence in court proceedings. The 11-month format is widely used specifically to avoid registration requirements and save on stamp duty.

title: Utility and Maintenance Charges
text: In standard Indian rental agreements, the tenant is responsible for paying utility bills such as electricity, water, and gas. Maintenance charges for the society or building are sometimes charged to the tenant, but this should be explicitly stated in the agreement. Clauses that include vague references to additional charges without specifying the amount or calculation formula are considered risky.

title: Tenant Rights to Peaceful Enjoyment
text: Every tenant in India has the right to peaceful enjoyment of the rented property throughout the tenancy period. The landlord cannot unreasonably interfere with the tenant's use of the property. Harassment by the landlord, such as repeated unannounced visits, shutting off utilities to force eviction, threatening calls, or using agents to intimidate tenants, is illegal. Under the Model Tenancy Act 2021, tenants can approach the Rent Authority for relief against landlord harassment.

title: Automatic Renewal Clauses
text: Some rental agreements include automatic renewal clauses that extend the agreement for another term if the tenant does not give notice to vacate by a specific deadline. These clauses can trap tenants into extended lock-in periods if they miss the notice window. A fair automatic renewal clause gives the tenant at least 30 days to provide notice before the renewal triggers.

title: Notice Period Norms
text: In India, the standard notice period for most roles is 30 to 90 days. For senior roles such as managers and above, 60 to 90 days is common. Under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act 1946, the probation period is typically 3 months, extendable to 6 months. For IT sector employees, 60 to 90 day notice periods are standard. Notice periods exceeding 90 days should be scrutinized. The notice period must be reciprocal unless there is a strong business justification.

title: Non-Compete Clause Enforceability
text: Non-compete clauses in India are generally considered unenforceable after the termination of employment. Under Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act 1872, any agreement that restrains a person from exercising a lawful profession, trade, or business is void to that extent. Courts have consistently held that post-employment non-compete restrictions are contrary to public policy. However, non-compete clauses during the period of employment are enforceable.

title: Gratuity Entitlement
text: Under the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, every employee who has completed 5 or more years of continuous service is entitled to gratuity. The formula is: 15 days salary for every completed year of service, calculated as (Last drawn salary x 15) divided by 26. The maximum gratuity payable is capped at Rs 20 lakh. Gratuity is a statutory right and cannot be waived by any contractual provision.

title: Probation Period Rules
text: Indian employment law does not prescribe a fixed maximum probation period, but the typical practice is 3 to 6 months. Under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act 1946 for covered establishments, the probation period is typically 3 months, extendable to 6 months. During probation, employers generally have broader discretion to terminate employment. However, termination during probation must still be for valid reasons and cannot be arbitrary or discriminatory.

title: NDA Scope and Enforceability
text: Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDAs) in Indian employment contracts are generally enforceable to the extent they protect legitimate business interests such as trade secrets, client lists, and proprietary technology. NDAs must define the confidential information with reasonable specificity. An overly broad NDA that prevents an employee from using their general skills and knowledge acquired during employment is unreasonable and may be challenged. NDAs are enforceable during employment and for a reasonable time after, typically 1 to 2 years.

title: PF and ESI Obligations
text: Under the Employees Provident Fund and Miscellaneous Provisions Act 1952, employers with 20 or more employees must enroll eligible employees in the PF scheme. The current PF contribution rate is 12 percent of basic salary from both employer and employee. Under the Employees State Insurance Act 1948, employers with 10 or more employees must provide ESI coverage for employees earning up to Rs 21,000 per month. ESI contributions are 3.25 percent from the employer and 0.75 percent from the employee.
act_name: Employees Provident Fund and Miscellaneous Provisions Act 1952

title: Wrongful Termination
text: In India, the grounds and process for terminating employment vary depending on whether the employee is a workman under the Industrial Disputes Act 1947 or a managerial employee. Workmen in establishments with 100 or more employees cannot be retrenched without government permission and must receive retrenchment compensation. For other employees, employment contracts typically include termination provisions with notice or payment in lieu of notice. Termination without following the contractual process or for discriminatory reasons can be challenged in Labor Courts.

title: Intellectual Property Assignment
text: Employment contracts in India commonly include clauses assigning intellectual property created by the employee during the course of employment to the employer. Under the Copyright Act 1957, works created by an employee in the course of their employment are owned by the employer unless otherwise agreed. Assignment clauses that extend to works created outside working hours and unrelated to employment are considered overreaching and may be partially unenforceable.

title: What Makes a Contract Valid in India
text: Under the Indian Contract Act 1872, a valid contract requires: an offer, acceptance, lawful consideration, parties competent to contract, free consent, and a lawful object. A contract is void if it is based on unlawful consideration, involves mistake of fact, or restrains legal proceedings. A contract is voidable if consent was obtained through coercion, undue influence, fraud, or misrepresentation. Both parties must be of legal age (18 years or above) and of sound mind.

title: Void and Voidable Clauses
text: Under the Indian Contract Act 1872, certain types of contractual clauses are void and unenforceable regardless of what the parties agree. These include: clauses that restrain marriage, clauses in restraint of trade or lawful profession (with limited exceptions), clauses that restrict legal proceedings, and wagering agreements. A void clause does not invalidate the entire contract unless the contract is indivisible from the void clause. Courts in India can strike down unconscionable or unreasonable clauses even in commercial contracts.

title: Penalty and Liquidated Damages Clauses
text: Indian courts draw a distinction between genuine pre-estimates of damage (liquidated damages) and penalties. Under Section 74 of the Indian Contract Act 1872, a party that suffers from a breach of contract is entitled to reasonable compensation regardless of whether a specific sum is named in the contract. Indian courts will reduce penalty clauses to reasonable compensation if the stipulated sum is grossly disproportionate to the actual loss suffered. Penalty clauses must be proportionate to the breach.

title: Arbitration Clauses
text: Arbitration clauses require parties to resolve disputes through private arbitration rather than courts. Under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996, arbitration agreements are generally enforceable and courts will refer disputes to arbitration when a valid arbitration clause exists. Arbitration can be more expensive than small claims court proceedings. Clauses specifying arbitration in a city different from where the agreement is being performed create practical difficulty and cost.

title: Indemnification Clauses
text: Indemnification clauses require one party to compensate the other for losses arising from specified events. Under Section 124 of the Indian Contract Act 1872, a contract of indemnity is valid and enforceable. However, overly broad indemnification clauses that require one party to indemnify the other for losses arising from the other party's own negligence or misconduct are considered unreasonable. Unlimited indemnification obligations create unbounded financial risk.

title: Unilateral Amendment Rights
text: Clauses that allow one party to unilaterally change the terms of a contract without the other party's consent are potentially void under Indian contract law. A valid contract requires mutual agreement, and unilateral changes to material terms without consent violate the principle of mutual assent. Courts in India have struck down clauses that give employers the right to unilaterally change pay, job roles, or other material terms without the employee's agreement.

title: One-Sided Termination Rights
text: A one-sided termination clause gives only one party (typically the landlord or employer) the right to terminate the agreement with short or no notice, while requiring the other party to give a longer notice period. This asymmetry creates significant risk for the weaker party. In rental agreements, a landlord who can terminate with 7 days notice while requiring 60 days notice from the tenant has disproportionate control. Such clauses should be resisted or negotiated to create equivalent termination rights for both parties.

title: Vague and Undefined Penalty Clauses
text: Penalty clauses that are vague, undefined, or leave the calculation of damages to the other party's discretion are particularly risky. Examples include: damages described as 'all costs and losses suffered' without a cap, penalties described as 'such amount as determined by the landlord', or indemnification for 'any loss howsoever caused'. These open-ended penalty provisions create unlimited financial exposure. Any penalty clause should specify the triggering event precisely, the method of calculation, and a maximum cap on liability.

title: Waiver of Legal Rights Clauses
text: Some contracts contain clauses where one party agrees to waive their right to sue, to file consumer complaints, or to approach any regulatory authority. Under Indian law, clauses that seek to waive statutory rights or rights available under consumer protection laws are generally void and unenforceable. A tenant cannot contractually waive their rights under the Model Tenancy Act, and an employee cannot waive their rights to PF, ESI, or gratuity. Clauses stating that the signing party agrees not to approach any court or authority for any reason are extremely aggressive and legally questionable.

title: Excessive Forfeiture Clauses
text: Forfeiture clauses specify circumstances in which deposited money will be forfeited entirely rather than returned. While some forfeiture clauses are standard (for example, forfeiting one month's rent if the tenant breaks the lock-in period), clauses that forfeit the entire security deposit for minor breaches or that allow forfeiture at the landlord's discretion are considered aggressive. Under Indian law, forfeiture provisions must bear a reasonable relationship to the actual loss suffered.

title: Sweeping NDA Clauses
text: Non-Disclosure Agreements with overly broad confidentiality clauses pose significant risk. Problem clauses include: defining confidential information as everything shared without requiring any marking as confidential, covering information that is publicly available or independently developed, preventing disclosure to legal advisors, and covering information for an unlimited duration. A reasonable NDA should define confidential information specifically, exclude public information, allow disclosure for legal compliance, and have a reasonable time limit of 2 to 5 years.

title: Consumer Forum Remedies
text: The Consumer Protection Act 2019 provides significant remedies to consumers in India, including for housing-related disputes. A tenant whose security deposit is wrongfully withheld may have recourse to Consumer Forums. Complaints can be filed in District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commissions for claims up to Rs 1 crore. The complaint must be filed within 2 years of the cause of action.

title: Civil Suit for Contract Breach
text: Any party to a contract in India can file a civil suit for breach of contract in an appropriate Civil Court. The relief available includes damages for the loss suffered, specific performance of the contract, and injunctions to prevent ongoing breach. A civil suit must be filed within 3 years of the breach under the Limitation Act 1963. Before filing a civil suit, sending a formal legal notice to the defaulting party is advisable.

title: Approaching the Rent Authority
text: Under the Model Tenancy Act 2021, state governments are required to establish Rent Authorities to resolve disputes between landlords and tenants. Tenants can approach the Rent Authority for issues including: unlawful eviction attempts, wrongful withholding of security deposits, landlord harassment, unauthorized rent increases, and failure to carry out essential repairs. The Rent Authority is intended to be a faster and more accessible dispute resolution mechanism than civil courts.

title: Labor Court Remedies
text: Employees in India have access to multiple forums for employment disputes. Labor Courts and Industrial Tribunals handle disputes regarding wrongful termination, non-payment of dues, and unfair labor practices for workmen covered under the Industrial Disputes Act 1947. For managerial and senior employees not covered under the Industrial Disputes Act, Civil Courts may have jurisdiction. The Payment of Wages Act 1936 provides fast-track remedies for non-payment of wages through Labor Commissioners.

title: Legal Notice as First Step
text: Before filing any legal complaint or court case in India, it is advisable and sometimes mandatory to send a formal legal notice to the other party. A legal notice puts the other party on formal notice of your grievance and demands specific action within a specified time period (typically 15 to 30 days). Sending a legal notice through an advocate adds weight to the communication and demonstrates seriousness. Many disputes are resolved at the legal notice stage without requiring court proceedings.

title: Importance of Reading Before Signing
text: Under Indian contract law, a person who signs a contract is generally bound by its terms even if they did not read it. Claims of not having read or understood the document are generally not a defense to enforcement unless there was fraud, misrepresentation, or undue influence. This makes it critically important to read every clause of a contract before signing. Once a contract is signed, changing its terms requires the agreement of both parties.

title: Learner Licence Requirements in India
text: In India, a learner licence is a provisional driving licence issued to a person learning to drive. Under the Motor Vehicles Act 1988 and the Central Motor Vehicles Rules 1989, a person must be at least 18 years of age to obtain a learner licence for motor vehicles other than motorcycles without gears. For motorcycles without gears (mopeds), the minimum age is 16 years with parental consent. To obtain a learner licence, the applicant must pass a written test on traffic rules, signs, and road safety. A learner licence is valid for 6 months from the date of issue. During this period, the learner must drive only under the supervision of a person holding a valid permanent driving licence. A learner licence holder must display an L plate on the vehicle while driving. Driving without a valid learner or permanent licence is an offence under Section 3 of the Motor Vehicles Act 1988.

title: Permanent Driving Licence Eligibility and Application Timeline
text: After obtaining a learner licence in India, a person can apply for a permanent driving licence subject to the following conditions under the Motor Vehicles Act 1988 and Central Motor Vehicles Rules 1989: The applicant must wait a minimum of 30 days after the issue date of the learner licence before applying for the permanent licence. The application for a permanent licence must be made within 6 months (180 days) of the learner licence issue date, because the learner licence itself is valid for only 6 months. If the permanent licence application is not made within this 6-month window, the applicant must obtain a fresh learner licence and begin again. To obtain the permanent licence, the applicant must pass a practical driving test at the Regional Transport Office (RTO). The driving test assesses the applicant's ability to drive safely, follow traffic rules, and handle the vehicle competently. Upon passing the test, the RTO issues a permanent driving licence which is a smartcard format in most states. The applicant must be medically fit to drive, certified by a registered medical practitioner for certain vehicle categories.

title: Driving Licence Validity and Renewal Rules in India
text: A permanent driving licence in India is valid for 20 years from the date of issue or until the holder reaches 50 years of age, whichever is earlier. After the age of 50, the licence must be renewed every 5 years. The licence can be renewed up to one year before its expiry. If the licence has already expired, it can be renewed within 5 years of expiry with a late fee. After 5 years of expiry, the holder may need to pass a fresh medical test or even a fresh driving test depending on the RTO. A driving licence covers the specific categories of vehicles (classes) for which it was issued, such as MCWOG (motorcycle without gear), MCWG (motorcycle with gear), LMV (light motor vehicle), HMV (heavy motor vehicle), and others. To add a new vehicle category, the holder must apply separately and may need to pass an additional test. Driving a vehicle category not covered by the licence is an offence.

title: Driving Without Licence and Traffic Offence Penalties
text: Under the Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act 2019, penalties for traffic offences in India were significantly increased. Driving without a valid licence (Section 3 read with Section 181) attracts a fine of Rs 5,000 and/or imprisonment of up to 3 months for a first offence. Driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs (Section 185) attracts a fine of Rs 10,000 and/or imprisonment of up to 6 months for a first offence, with enhanced penalties for repeat offences. Over-speeding (Section 183) attracts a fine of Rs 1,000 to Rs 2,000 depending on vehicle type. Using a mobile phone while driving (Section 184) attracts a fine of Rs 1,000 to Rs 5,000. Jumping a red signal (Section 177A) attracts a fine of Rs 1,000 to Rs 5,000. Not wearing a seatbelt (Section 194B) attracts a fine of Rs 1,000. Not wearing a helmet (Section 129 read with Section 194D) attracts a fine of Rs 1,000 and a 3-month licence suspension for a repeat offence. These fines apply per offence and can be compounded by police or RTO officers.

title: Vehicle Registration and Fitness Certificate Requirements
text: Every motor vehicle in India must be registered under the Motor Vehicles Act 1988 before it is used on a public road. Registration is done at the Regional Transport Office (RTO) of the owner's district. A new vehicle is issued a temporary registration for 1 month while the permanent registration is processed. The permanent registration certificate (RC) is valid for 15 years for non-transport vehicles (private cars, motorcycles) and 10 years for transport vehicles. After the initial validity period, the RC must be renewed and the vehicle must obtain a fitness certificate. A fitness certificate certifies that the vehicle meets road safety standards. Transport vehicles must obtain a fitness certificate annually. Driving an unregistered vehicle or a vehicle without a valid fitness certificate is an offence under the Motor Vehicles Act. Vehicle insurance is also mandatory for all vehicles — driving without third-party insurance is a criminal offence under Section 196 of the Motor Vehicles Act.

title: Challan Process and Motor Vehicle Disputes
text: When a traffic violation is detected in India, a challan (fine notice) is issued by the traffic police or transport authority. Challans can be issued on the spot (physical) or electronically (e-challan) through camera-based detection systems. The vehicle owner or driver must pay the challan amount at a police station, traffic court, or online through the state's transport portal within the specified time, typically 60 to 90 days. If the challan is not paid, a court summons may be issued and the case may be treated as a compoundable or non-compoundable offence. Compoundable offences can be settled by paying the fine. Non-compoundable offences (such as driving under the influence causing injury or death) must go through a full court trial. A driver can contest a challan by appearing before the Motor Accidents Claims Tribunal or the Judicial Magistrate's Court within the time limit specified on the challan. The driver has the right to produce evidence and argue that the challan was wrongly issued.

title: What is Anticipatory Bail in India
text: Anticipatory bail is a direction by the Sessions Court or High Court to release a person on bail in the event of their arrest, granted before the arrest actually happens. It is governed by Section 438 of the Code of Criminal Procedure 1973 (CrPC). A person who apprehends arrest for a non-bailable offence can apply for anticipatory bail. The court considers factors including the nature and gravity of the accusation, the antecedents of the applicant, and the possibility of the applicant fleeing from justice. Anticipatory bail can be granted with conditions such as making oneself available for interrogation, surrendering the passport, or not leaving the country. If anticipatory bail is granted and the person is subsequently arrested, they must be released immediately on bail. A chargesheet being filed does not automatically cancel the anticipatory bail — the court must be separately approached. Anticipatory bail can be cancelled by the court that granted it if the conditions are violated or if new circumstances arise.

title: Bail in India — Regular Bail and Types
text: Bail is a legal mechanism in India by which an arrested person is temporarily released from custody on the condition that they will appear before the court when required. There are three main types of bail in India. Regular bail (Section 437 and 439 CrPC) is applied for after arrest, before or during trial. Bail for bailable offences is a right — the police must grant it. For non-bailable offences, bail is discretionary and granted by a magistrate or sessions court. Interim bail is a temporary bail granted for a short period while the full bail application is being heard. Anticipatory bail (Section 438 CrPC) is granted before arrest when a person anticipates being arrested. The Supreme Court or High Court can grant bail under Article 32 or 226 of the Constitution in exceptional circumstances. Bail is generally not granted in offences punishable with death or life imprisonment unless there are exceptional reasons. Bail conditions typically include surrender of passport, regular reporting to police, and not leaving the jurisdiction.

title: Property Registration Requirements in India
text: Under the Registration Act 1908, the Transfer of Property Act 1882, and the Indian Stamp Act 1899, the purchase or transfer of immovable property in India must be registered at the Sub-Registrar's office in whose jurisdiction the property is located. A sale deed must be executed on non-judicial stamp paper of appropriate value (as per the circle rate of the property) and registered within 4 months of execution. If registration is delayed, the Sub-Registrar can allow late registration with a penalty within 8 months. An unregistered sale deed cannot be used as evidence of title in court proceedings. Before registration, the buyer must verify the title of the property through a title search for at least 30 years. Property mutation (change of ownership in revenue records) must be done separately at the municipal or revenue authority after registration. Gift deeds for immovable property must also be compulsorily registered. A will for property does not require registration but registration is advisable for legal strength.

title: Consumer Rights and Complaints in India
text: The Consumer Protection Act 2019 provides comprehensive protection to consumers in India. A consumer is any person who buys goods or avails services for consideration. Consumer rights include the right to safety, right to information, right to choose, right to be heard, right to seek redressal, and right to consumer education. Consumers can file complaints with the District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (DCDRC) for claims up to Rs 50 lakh, State Commission for claims between Rs 50 lakh and Rs 2 crore, and National Commission for claims above Rs 2 crore. A consumer complaint must be filed within 2 years of the cause of action. The complaint can be filed by the consumer directly without a lawyer. Relief available includes replacement of goods, refund of price, removal of deficiency, compensation for loss, and punitive damages. E-commerce platforms, online transactions, and digital services are also covered under the Act. Misleading advertisements causing harm to consumers are a punishable offence under the 2019 Act.

title: Minimum Wages and Wage Payment Rights in India
text: The Minimum Wages Act 1948 requires employers to pay workers at least the minimum wage fixed by the government for their category of work. Minimum wages are set by state governments for different scheduled employments and are revised periodically. The Payment of Wages Act 1936 regulates the time and mode of wage payment — wages must be paid by the 7th of the following month for establishments with fewer than 1000 workers and by the 10th for others. Deductions from wages are permitted only for specific reasons such as absence from duty, house accommodation provided by employer, advances given, and income tax. Unauthorised deductions from wages are illegal. An employee whose wages are wrongfully withheld can file a complaint before the Payment of Wages Authority appointed by the state government. Remedies include payment of the withheld wages plus interest and compensation up to 10 times the withheld amount for malicious delay. Under the Labour Codes enacted in 2019-2020 (currently pending state-wise implementation), the minimum wage framework is being simplified into a floor wage and state minimum wage structure.

title: Cybercrime and IT Act Provisions in India
text: The Information Technology Act 2000 (IT Act) and its 2008 amendments are the primary laws governing cybercrime and digital transactions in India. Key provisions include: Section 66 — hacking or damaging computer systems is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and/or fine up to Rs 5 lakh. Section 66C — identity theft (using someone's electronic signature, password, or unique identification without authorisation) is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine up to Rs 1 lakh. Section 66D — cheating by impersonation using a computer resource is punishable similarly. Section 67 — publishing obscene material in electronic form is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine up to Rs 5 lakh. Section 43 — unauthorised access to a computer network results in liability to pay compensation to the affected person. Cybercrime complaints can be filed at the nearest cybercrime police station or online at cybercrime.gov.in. The Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) handles cyber incident reporting. Online financial fraud should also be reported to bank helplines immediately and to the RBI ombudsman if unresolved.
